turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: I have the opinion of the court in case 16-327, Lee versus United States. Thirty-five years ago, petitioner, Jae Lee moved to this country from South Korea, he was 13 at that time. His family settled in New York City, where Lee attended school, eventually graduating from a business high school in Manhattan. After graduation, he set off on his own heading to Memphis, Tennessee, where he started working in a restaurant. After three years there, Lee decided to try his hand at running a business. With some assistance from his family, he opened the Mandarin Palace Chinese Restaurant. The Mandarin was a success and Lee eventually opened a second restaurant nearby. During the 30 plus years, he has spent in this country, Lee has never returned to South Korea, nor has he become a United States citizen, living instead as a lawful permanent resident. Now, while a successful businessman, Lee also engaged in some illegal activity. In 2009, acting on a tip from a confidential informant, federal officials searched Lee's house and found among other things, 88 ecstasy pills which Lee admitted were his. Lee was subsequently charged with one count of possessing ecstasy with intent to distribute. Lee hired an attorney and entered in to plea negotiations with the Government. During those discussions, Lee informed his attorney that he was not a United States citizen and repeatedly asked him whether he would be deported, because of his drug charge. The attorney assured Lee there was nothing to worry about; he would not be deported if he pleaded guilty. He would however, the attorney said, “Get a lighter sentence.” So Lee, who really had no good defense to the charge against him, pleaded guilty. Lee soon learned that his attorney was dead wrong. Lee had pleaded guilty to what is known as an aggravated felony and non-citizens convicted of such crimes are subject to mandatory deportation from this country. Upon realizing he would be deported because of his guilty plea, Lee filed a post conviction motion to vacate his conviction and sentence on the ground that he had received constitutionally ineffective assistance of counsel. Now, the Government conceives that Lee's attorney behaved deficiently by misinforming him about the deportation consequences of pleading guilty. The question before us is whether Lee can show he was prejudiced as a result of that bad advice. We established the test for answering this question 30 years ago, in a case called Hill against Lockhart. As we explained in that case, a defendant who argues that his council's deficient performance led him to accept the guilty plea rather than go to trial can establish prejudice. He can do that by showing a reasonable probability that, but for his council's errors, he would have gone to trial. So, the question today is whether if Lee's council had told him that he would be deported if he pleaded guilty, Lee would have gone to trial rather than accept the plea. We are convinced that the answer is yes, Lee would have taken his chances at trial rather than agree to a plea that would automatically lead to his deportation. It is undisputed that deportation was the determinative issue in Lee's decision about whether to accept or reject the plea. In fact, when Lee was formally entering his plea in Court, the judge warned him that doing so, could result in deportation and asked, “Does that at all affect to your decision about whether you want to plead guilty or not?”, Lee answered, “Yes, your honor.” When the judge followed up by asking, “How does it affect your decision?” Lee responded, “I don't understand”, and turned to his attorney. The same one who had told him he would not be deported if he pleaded guilty for advice. Only when Lee's council assured him that the judge's statement was just a standard warning, was Lee willing to proceed to plead guilty, that Lee placed so much emphasis on deportation makes sense. At the time of his plea, Lee had lived in the United States for nearly three decades and established two businesses in Tennessee; had not been to Korea since he was 13, and was the only family member in the United States who could care for his elderly parents, both naturalized American citizens. The Government, however, argues that a defendant cannot show he was prejudiced by accepting a plea bargain, unless he can show that the decision to reject the plea would have been rational. And the Government says, “Lee cannot make that showing. He had no good defense to the charge against him and would very likely lose if he went to trial.” The end result of that would still be deportation, with a longer prison sentence to boot. In the Government's view, accepting a plea that resulted in less time in prison before deportation was the only rational choice. Now, the Government is right that if Lee went to trial, he almost certainly would have been convicted and been subject to deportation. But almost certainly, is not the same as certainly, and if staying in the United States is the determinative consideration, that almost can make all the difference. If Lee had gone to trial, there would have been some chance, a small chance, but a chance nonetheless that he would not have been convicted and thus, that he would not have been deported. The cost to holding on to this small chance of avoiding deportation was a year or two more of prison time that Lee would face from a conviction at trial as opposed to the plea. Not everyone in Lee's position would make the choice to risk more jail time for a small shot at avoiding deportation, but we cannot say it would be irrational to do so. We conclude, that Lee has demonstrated a reasonable probability that, but for his attorney's very bad advice, he would have rejected the Government's plea and gone to trial. And we therefore hold, that he has shown he was prejudiced by his attorney's incompetence. The judgment to the contrary of the Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit is reversed and the case is remanded for further proceedings. Justice Thomas has filed a descending opinion in which Justice Alito has joined. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
